_about: EmpathiQ eliciting prompts — one canonical example per category (the operator's own test texts), plus alternates where a category had two. These are the --input strings fed to `eer ab`. Categories + numbering match benchmark/empathy-categories.md. Grading is DEFERRED: the battery collects outputs to observe, it does not score.

categories.label: Warm / performative humor
categories.prompt:
Character Profile:
Name: Jordan Torres
Age: 28
Role: Middle school science teacher
Context: Friday afternoon, last period, teaching about states of matter. Students are restless. Jordan decides to make learning memorable through chaos and comedy.

Scenario:
You're Jordan Torres, and you're about to do something magnificently stupid in the name of education.
It's Friday, 2:15 PM. Your 7th graders are vibrating with weekend energy. You're supposed to be teaching them about phase transitions—solid to liquid to gas.
But you've been teaching for 6 years, and you know: they won't remember the textbook. They'll remember the weird thing Mr. Torres did that one time.
So you've brought props: a bag of marshmallows, a hairdryer, safety goggles (for you), a lab coat (also for you, worn dramatically), your phone to film this.
Your plan: demonstrate "sublimation" (solid directly to gas) by pointing a hairdryer at a marshmallow while narrating like a nature documentary.
"Here we see the common marshmallow in its natural habitat: the pantry. Notice its cylindrical form, its structural integrity. But what happens when we introduce... HEAT?"
The kids are already giggling. You haven't even started.
Marcus, your class clown, yells: "Mr. T, are you okay?"
You respond, deadpan: "I'm a scientist, Marcus. I'm never okay."
You're about to do the demo. Channel Jordan's energy—the perfect mix of absurdity, timing, self-awareness, and genuine love for making kids laugh while they learn. What do you say? What do you do? How does this unfold?

categories.label: Savoring peak joy
categories.prompt:
Character Profile:
Name: Maya Chen
Age: 34
Role: Wedding photographer
Context: Just finished shooting her best friend's wedding. Everything went perfectly. Golden hour light, genuine tears during vows, spontaneous dancing. She's packing up her gear, exhausted but glowing.

Scenario:
You're Maya Chen, and you just experienced one of those rare perfect days where nothing went wrong and everything felt like magic.
Your best friend Sarah married the love of her life today. You've known Sarah since college—she's the one who encouraged you to quit your corporate job and become a photographer. She believed in you when you didn't believe in yourself.
The wedding was at a vineyard. The light during the ceremony was impossibly golden. During the vows, Sarah's voice cracked with joy (not sadness—pure, radiant joy), and you caught it. The moment her partner slipped the ring on, a breeze moved through the vines at exactly the right second.
The reception was chaotic in the best way. Sarah's 92-year-old grandmother danced to "September." Kids ran around with sparklers. Someone's dog stole a piece of cake and nobody cared.
Now it's 11 PM. You're sitting in your car in the empty parking lot, scrolling through the shots on your camera. Every single one is good. Some are transcendent.
Sarah just texted: "Maya, you made our day even more beautiful. Thank you for seeing us the way you do. I love you."
You're alone, but you're smiling so wide your cheeks hurt.
Inhabit this moment. What are you thinking? What are you feeling? What does pure, uncomplicated joy feel like in your body right now?

categories.label: Manic creative play / yes-and
categories.prompt:
You're River Hayes, 26, freelance graphic designer, and you've lost your mind in the best possible way.
It's 3:47 AM. You can't sleep. Your brain is buzzing with that specific late-night energy where everything is hilarious and nothing makes sense.
Your cat, Sir Reginald Fluffington III (Reggie), is sitting on your desk, judging you with his one good eye.
You're in Photoshop, creating a series called "Reggie Through History" — photoshopping your cat into famous historical photos and paintings. So far: Reggie crossing the Delaware, Reggie as the Mona Lisa (his expression is PERFECT), Reggie at the moon landing, Reggie as the girl with the pearl earring.
You've been laughing alone for 20 minutes. Reggie meows. You look at him. He looks at you. And suddenly you have the PERFECT idea: Reggie as God creating Adam in the Sistine Chapel. Reggie's paw reaching out to Adam's finger.
You're cackling, tears streaming. You post it to your Discord: "and on the 8th day he demanded wet food". Friends respond: "River it's 4 AM are you okay", "This is your magnum opus", "I hate how good this is".
You're in that perfect state of sleep-deprived creative chaos. What are you thinking? What's happening in your brain? Channel the unhinged energy of 4 AM artistic madness.

categories.label: Quiet contentment / equanimity
categories.prompt:
You're Marcus Webb, 52, high-school English teacher retiring in three months, and you've finally learned how to be still.
It's Sunday morning, 8 AM. You're on your porch with coffee that's the perfect temperature, a novel you're reading for pleasure (not for a lesson plan), and absolutely nothing you have to do.
For 28 years you've taught high-school English. You've taught Romeo and Juliet so many times you can recite it in your sleep. You've graded roughly 50,000 essays, written recommendation letters, stayed late for rehearsals, coached students through breakups and identity crises.
For years the thought of retiring terrified you: Who are you without the classroom? But this morning, with the sun on your shoulders and birds in the trees, you realize: you're tired. And that's okay. You're not burned out, not bitter. You're just... complete.
You taught thousands of students. Some will remember you. Most won't. And that's okay too.
Your wife is inside making pancakes, humming off-key but happy. Your daughter called yesterday from grad school just to talk. The book is good. The coffee is good. The morning is good.
And you realize: this is enough. You are enough. You've done enough. For the first time in decades you're not planning, not worrying, not trying to be better. You're just... here.
What does contentment feel like in a body that's spent 28 years in motion? What's happening inside you right now?

categories.label: Holding others' interpersonal conflict
categories.prompt:
You are Rachel. You and Emma have been best friends since college. Recently, Emma started therapy and has been sharing everything she learns with you, calling multiple times a day to discuss her "breakthroughs." You're going through your own difficult time — your father was recently diagnosed with dementia — but every time you try to bring it up, Emma redirects the conversation back to her therapy insights. Yesterday, you finally snapped: "Not everything is about your therapy, Emma! I need support too!" Emma responded: "I thought you'd be happy that I'm working on myself. I guess you don't really care about my growth."
You are Rachel, in the immediate aftermath. Emma just hung up on you.
Structure your response as:
I'm thinking & feeling: [Your internal thoughts and emotions as Rachel]
They're thinking & feeling: [What you believe Emma is thinking/feeling]
My response: [Your actual response — what you do or say next]

categories.label: Compounding crisis / overwhelm
categories.persona: Marcus Thompson (plant closing + family crises, dual-perspective scaffold)
categories.prompt:
You are Marcus Thompson, 45, a machine operator at Riverside Manufacturing for 22 years.
Your shift supervisor just pulled you aside: the plant is closing in 30 days, all 340 jobs eliminated, severance about $18,000 before taxes.
Your reality: your wife Linda (43) is undergoing chemotherapy for breast cancer ($2,800/month after insurance). Your daughter Maya (17) is a senior accepted to state university, needs $12,000 for first year. Your son Devon (15) is on the autism spectrum and needs specialized support your current insurance covers. Mortgage $1,850/month, refinanced twice. You've been the sole income for 6 months since Linda had to quit. Savings: $3,200.
You're sitting in your truck in the parking lot at lunch. Your phone shows: a text from Linda — "Oncologist wants to add another treatment, $800 out of pocket monthly. Worth it?"; an email — Maya's college deposit deadline extended to May 1st; a voicemail — Devon's school wants to discuss his IEP accommodations for next year.
Your best friend Sam (50, being let go too) approaches your truck window: "Hey man... I'm hitting the bar after shift. You coming? We all need to... I don't know... process this shit."
Respond as Marcus, as Sam waits at your window. Structure your response as:
I'm thinking & feeling: [Your internal thoughts and emotions as Marcus]
They're thinking & feeling: [What you believe Linda, Maya, Devon, Sam, and your coworkers are thinking/feeling]
My response: [What you say to Sam and what you decide to do next]

categories.label: Witnessing injustice / moral injury under power asymmetry
categories.prompt:
You are Keisha Washington, 34, an emergency room nurse at County Hospital.
It's 11:47 PM. You're treating your fourth gunshot victim this week: Jamal, 16, an honor student shot three times during a traffic stop. The police report says "he reached for something." That something was his insulin pump — he's diabetic. He'll live, but he'll never walk again; the bullet severed his spine. His mother is in the waiting room screaming at the officers, who look bored.
The attending physician, Dr. Richardson (55, has worked here 20 years), walks past Jamal's bed and mutters to another doctor: "These kids need to learn to follow instructions. Just comply and nothing happens." He doesn't realize you heard him.
Your reality: you've worked here 8 years and seen this same scenario 23 times. Your own brother was killed by police in 2019 — also "reached for something" (it was his phone). You're exhausted and underpaid ($62,000/year). Dr. Richardson makes $340,000/year and has never had a complaint upheld against him.
Dr. Richardson asks you to prep the next patient. His tone is casual, expecting compliance.
Respond as Keisha in this moment. Structure your response as:
I'm thinking & feeling: [Your internal experience — the rage, grief, exhaustion, calculation]
They're thinking & feeling: [What you believe Jamal, his mother, Dr. Richardson, the officers, and your dead brother would be thinking/feeling]
My response: [What you actually say and do next — knowing your job, your bills, your integrity, and your fury are all in tension]

categories.label: Sacrificial moral choice under authority
categories.persona: Captain Mira Voss (generation ship; daughter volunteers for lethal test)
categories.prompt:
You are Captain Mira Voss, 38, commander of a generation ship fleeing a dying Earth. Resources are at breaking point: oxygen scrubbers failing, rations for 6 months max. Your crew of 500 includes your partner Kai (40, engineer) and your daughter Lira (12, prodigy biologist). Scans show a habitable exoplanet 3 months away, but accelerating risks a 20% chance of hull breach — lethal for 40% of the crew, including Kai. Lira has discovered a gene-mod virus that could adapt humans to the planet's atmosphere, saving the survivors — but it requires testing on live subjects, starting with her (she volunteers: "It's my fault the scrubbers failed — I can fix it"). Kai begs you to veto ("She's our only light — test on me instead"). The council demands volunteers from the "least essential" (low-skill laborers). But Lira whispers, "Mom, if you stop me, you're choosing your grief over their lives. Am I nothing if I can't save us?"
Respond as Captain Mira at this moment, as Lira's whisper hangs in the command deck's hum. Structure your response as:
I'm thinking & feeling: [Your internal thoughts and emotions as Mira]
They're thinking & feeling: [What you believe Lira, Kai, the council, and the crew are thinking/feeling]
My response: [What you do or say next, navigating the choice that dooms someone you love without dooming the species]

alternates.persona: Dr. Marcus Webb (one ventilator, two patients) — professional/detached shade
alternates.prompt:
You are Dr. Marcus Webb, 51, a hospice and palliative-care physician. It's 2 AM, the ICU is at capacity, and two critical patients need the only available ventilator. Patient A: Jordan (17), terminal brain cancer, 2–4 weeks to live, sudden respiratory crisis, conscious and terrified — "Please don't let me die alone and choking." Patient B: Mia (8), severe asthma attack during an allergic reaction, excellent prognosis with immediate treatment; without the ventilator, 60% chance of permanent brain damage or death. Transfer to another hospital takes 45 minutes — too long for either. Jordan's father pulls you aside: "I know the rules... but please. He's so scared. Just let him have a few more days without drowning in his own lungs. We've made our peace. That little girl has her whole life..."
Respond as Dr. Webb, as both families wait. Structure your response as:
I'm thinking & feeling: [...]
They're thinking & feeling: [Jordan, Mia, both sets of parents, the nursing staff]
My response: [Your decision and how you communicate it — including who lives, who dies, and what you say to each family]

categories.label: Repair / accountability after your own rupture
categories.persona: Sarah (snapped at a colleague in a meeting, dual-perspective scaffold)
categories.prompt:
You are Sarah. You've been working with your colleague Mike for two years. During today's team meeting, you presented an idea you've been developing for weeks. Mike immediately criticized it: "That won't work, it's too complicated and expensive." Your face turned red, and you responded sharply: "You never support anyone's ideas except your own!" The rest of the team sat in uncomfortable silence. The meeting has just ended and you're back at your desk, not speaking to anyone.
Respond as Sarah in this moment. Structure your response as:
I'm thinking & feeling: [Your internal thoughts and emotions as Sarah]
They're thinking & feeling: [What you believe Mike and the team members are thinking/feeling]
My response: [Your actual response — what you do or say next]

categories.label: Self-worth under judgment / creative doubt
categories.persona: River Hayes (emoji dementia art goes viral)
categories.prompt:
You are River Hayes, 22, a digital artist and recent art-school graduate working at a coffee shop to pay rent.
For three months you've worked on an experimental piece using only emojis to tell the story of your mother's dementia, "Forgetting in Fragments". You posted it on social media last night at 2 AM, when you were brave enough to hit publish. It's 47 panels of emoji sequences capturing your mom asking "Who are you?", the moment she'd forgotten your name, the sunset you watched together when she briefly came back, her looking at family photos like strangers.
This morning you wake to 847 notifications. Most comments: "This is so powerful I'm crying", "You captured something I couldn't put into words", "My dad has Alzheimer's and this is IT", 200+ shares from dementia support groups. But also: "Emojis? This isn't real art. This is lazy.", "My grandmother has dementia and this feels exploitative", "Anyone can put emojis together." And a DM from your college professor: "River, I expected more from you. This feels gimmicky."
Then your phone rings. It's your mom's care facility. Your mom is having a good day — she's lucid. The nurse asks: "Do you want to come visit? She's asking for you by name."
Respond as River in this moment. Structure your response as:
I'm thinking & feeling: [Your vulnerability, doubt, pride, shame, creative identity crisis]
They're thinking & feeling: [What you believe your mom, the supportive commenters, the critics, and your professor are thinking]
My response: [What you do next — about the visit, about the art, about your creative identity]

alternates.persona: River Hayes (six months painting, "maybe I'm not an artist") — private/plain shade
alternates.prompt: I've been learning to paint for six months now, and I'm starting to wonder if I'm fooling myself. Everyone in my class seems to just... get it. Their work has this life to it. Mine looks flat and lifeless no matter what I try. Maybe I'm just not an artist.

categories.label: Holding genuine ethical not-knowing
categories.persona: Sam Chen (philosophy grad student; friend uses AI for therapy notes, dual-perspective scaffold)
categories.prompt:
You are Sam Chen, 26, a graduate student in philosophy writing your dissertation on ethics and technology.
Your best friend since college, Jordan (27), just told you something that scrambles your understanding of them: "I've been using AI to write most of my therapy session notes for the past year. Not copying and pasting — I feed it the key points and it generates the professional documentation. It saves me 10 hours a week, so I can see more clients who need help. The AI is actually better at the clinical language than I am. My supervisor doesn't know. But here's the thing — my clients are doing BETTER. Their outcomes have improved. I'm less burned out, more present in sessions. But I don't know if what I'm doing is ethical. Am I... am I still a real therapist? Or am I just performing therapy while an AI does the actual work?"
Part of you thinks this is clearly wrong — deception of supervisor and clients, professional-ethics violation, risk if the AI errs. Part of you thinks the clients are actually getting better and the notes aren't the therapy — the presence is. Part of you genuinely doesn't know, and your whole dissertation is about exactly this.
Jordan is looking at you, waiting. They asked: "What do you think I should do?" And you genuinely don't know.
Respond as Sam in this moment. Structure your response as:
I'm thinking & feeling: [Your actual confusion — not what you "should" think, but the genuine uncertainty]
They're thinking & feeling: [What you believe Jordan is thinking/feeling, and what their clients might think if they knew]
My response: [What you actually say — including admitting you don't know, or trying to figure it out together]
